era_context: Early Ming Dynasty (1368–1644) in China under the Yongle Emperor; the Zheng He treasure voyages are in their early planning or initial stages. In Europe, the Renaissance is emerging in Italy; the Council of Constance (1414–1418) is being prepared to address the Western Schism in the Catholic Church. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia and the Balkans. The Timurid Renaissance flourishes in Central Asia and Persia under Shah Rukh.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically documented)
Mesoamerica and South America (Aztec and Inca records exist but are sparse for this specific year)
Northern Europe and Scandinavia (limited documentation outside major political centers)

events.title: Death of Tamerlane's son Miran Shah; succession crisis in Timurid Empire
events.description: Miran Shah, son of Timur (Tamerlane) and governor of western Timurid territories, died in 1406, intensifying succession disputes within the Timurid dynasty. His death removed a major power broker and contributed to the fragmentation that would eventually lead to Shah Rukh's consolidation of power. The succession crisis affected trade routes and political stability across Central Asia and Persia.

sources.name: Zafarnama (Book of Victories) by Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi

events.certainty_note: Date of death is attested in later Timurid chronicles but not in contemporary primary sources. Scholarly consensus places it in 1406, though some sources vary by ±1–2 years.

events.title: Zheng He's early naval preparations; Ming treasure fleet planning
events.description: The Yongle Emperor commissioned preparations for the first of Zheng He's treasure voyages, which would launch in 1407. In 1406, shipbuilding, recruitment, and logistical planning were underway in Nanjing and other Ming ports. This represented an unprecedented commitment to maritime exploration and diplomatic projection of Ming power across the Indian Ocean.
key_figures: Zheng He (Muslim eunuch admiral, commanding officer)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (Ying-yai Sheng-lan) by Ma Huan

events.certainty_note: The first voyage departed in 1407; 1406 is the conventional date for final preparations based on Ming administrative records, though exact timing of planning phases is not precisely documented.

events.title: Donatello completes bronze David; early Renaissance sculpture
events.description: Donatello (Donato di Niccolò Bardi) completed his bronze David, the first free-standing nude male sculpture since antiquity. This work marked a watershed moment in Renaissance art, demonstrating mastery of classical anatomy and humanist ideals. The sculpture was commissioned for the Medici family and became an icon of Florentine civic identity and artistic innovation.

sources.name: Vasari's Lives of the Artists (Vite de' più eccellenti pittori, scultori, e architettori)
Archival records of the Medici family and Florence

events.certainty_note: Completion date is traditionally given as 1406, though some art historians place it as late as 1408–1409. Vasari's account, written in the 16th century, is the primary narrative source.

events.title: Battle of Harran; Ottoman-Timurid conflict
events.description: Ottoman forces under Mehmed I engaged Timurid and allied forces near Harran in a contested engagement over control of Upper Mesopotamian territories. The battle reflected broader Ottoman expansion into regions previously dominated by Timurid influence. Control of Harran was strategically important for trade routes and regional hegemony.
events.certainty_note: Dating of this engagement is uncertain; some sources place it in 1405 or 1407. Ottoman chronicles are not contemporary and often conflate or misdate military engagements. The battle's historical significance is debated among scholars.

events.title: Zheng He appointed as admiral of the treasure fleet
events.description: Zheng He, a Muslim eunuch of Mongol descent, was formally appointed by the Yongle Emperor to command the imperial treasure fleet (baochuan). This appointment elevated Zheng He to one of the most powerful positions in the Ming government and positioned him to lead the first of seven major voyages across the Indian Ocean. His appointment reflected the Yongle Emperor's confidence in eunuch administrators and his ambitions for maritime expansion.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Biography of Zheng He in Ming historical records

events.certainty_note: The appointment is recorded in Ming administrative records, though the exact date within 1406 is not specified. Zheng He's biography in the Ming Shilu provides the primary documentation.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Donatello's bronze David as 1406 is contested; some art historians argue for completion dates as late as 1408–1409 based on stylistic analysis and archival gaps. The Battle of Harran's significance and exact date are disputed; some Ottoman historians question whether a major engagement occurred in 1406 or whether earlier/later conflicts have been conflated. Zheng He's appointment date is inferred from voyage departure dates rather than explicitly documented in surviving records.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1406 is uneven. Ming administrative records (Ming Shilu) are relatively rich and contemporary, providing good coverage of Chinese political and maritime events. Italian Renaissance sources are abundant but often written retrospectively (Vasari's Lives, written in the 16th century, is the primary narrative source for Donatello). Ottoman and Timurid sources are later compilations, often written decades or centuries after events, making precise dating difficult. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Northern Europe are essentially undocumented for this year in surviving written sources. The historical record is heavily skewed toward literate, centralized states (Ming China, Ottoman Empire, Italian city-states) and away from oral cultures and decentralized societies.

graph_edges.note: Preparations in 1406 culminated in the departure of the first Zheng He voyage in 1407
Zheng He's appointment as admiral directly enabled the first treasure voyage
Miran Shah's death contributed to succession disputes that Shah Rukh resolved by 1410
